Name: traffic-ticket-contest
Description: A production-grade ERB platform (admin/state-machine/jurisdiction/routing/feature-catalog machinery, extracted from the industrial-ui-services portal) with traffic-ticket contest as the example domain. The platform tables carry the full production conventions; tickets are one domain riding on top.

_meta._CMCC_Summary: Airtable export with schema-first type mapping: Schemas, Data, Relationships (FK links), Lookups (INDEX/MATCH), Aggregations (SUMIFS/COUNTIFS/Rollups), and Calculated fields (formulas) in Excel dialect. Field types are determined from Airtable's schema metadata FIRST (no coercion), with intelligent fallback to formula/data analysis only when schema is unavailable.

_conversion_metadata.field_type_mapping: checkbox→boolean, number→number/integer, multipleRecordLinks→relationship, multipleLookupValues→lookup, rollup→aggregation, count→aggregation, formula→calculated

type_inference.priority: airtable_metadata (NO COERCION) → formula_analysis → reference_resolution → data_analysis (fallback only)
type_inference.airtable_type_mapping: checkbox→boolean, singleLineText→string, multilineText→string, number→number/integer, datetime→datetime, singleSelect→string, email→string, url→string, phoneNumber→string
type_inference.data_coercion_hierarchy: Only used as fallback when Airtable schema unavailable: datetime → number → integer → boolean → base64 → json → string

data.Title: Citation requires driver, violation and jurisdiction
data.Description: A citation must reference exactly one driver, one violation type, and one issuing jurisdiction.

data.Title: Response due date is derived from the jurisdiction
data.Description: ResponseDueDate = IssuedOn + the issuing jurisdiction's DaysToRespond. Changing the jurisdiction's rule re-derives every citation under it.

data.Title: A response is pay or contest
data.Description: Responding means either paying the fine or requesting a hearing; either sets RespondedOn and advances the citation to Responded.

data.Title: Citation status is computed, never stored
data.Description: CitationStatus is re-derived on every read from raw facts and child events; there is no stored status column to drift.

data.Title: No response by deadline is a default judgment
data.Description: If the response window passes with no RespondedOn, the citation moves to Adjudicated by default.

data.Title: Contest status follows the latest hearing
data.Description: ContestStatus is derived from the newest related hearing: NotContested | HearingRequested → Scheduled → Heard.

data.Title: Only a manager schedules a hearing
data.Description: Transitioning a hearing from HearingRequested to Scheduled is a manager-role action.

data.Title: Fine is the violation's base fine
data.Description: The amount owed starts at the violation type's BaseFineUsd for the citation's jurisdiction.

data.Title: Late payment incurs a jurisdiction penalty
data.Description: If payment is not made by the pay-by date, a penalty of LatePenaltyPct of the fine is added.

data.Title: Prolonged non-payment goes to collections
data.Description: A Late citation becomes Collections after DaysLateToCollections days past the pay-by date.

data.Title: A dismissed citation owes nothing
data.Description: If a hearing dismisses the citation, PaymentStatus is NotOwed regardless of the fine.

data.Title: Active points roll up to the driver
data.Description: A driver's ActivePoints is the sum of points from adjudicated, non-dismissed citations.

data.Title: Points drive license warning then suspension
data.Description: LicenseStatus is Warning at the jurisdiction's PointWarningThreshold and Suspended at PointSuspensionThreshold.

data.Title: Regulations are data, not code
data.Description: Every regulatory knob — deadlines, fines, penalties, thresholds, school caps — is a column on Jurisdictions, editable without code changes.

data.Title: Overdue work is urgent
data.Description: A work-queue item whose due date is in the past is urgent and sorts to the top.

data.Title: Work items mirror their subject's deadline
data.Description: A work item's DueDate is carried from its subject (e.g. Citations.ResponseDueDate or PaymentDueDate).

data.Title: Overrides require a reason
data.Description: Any OVERRIDE audit entry must carry a non-empty Reason; the trail is append-only.

data.Title: Every assistant turn is costed
data.Description: Each assistant turn records input/output tokens and the exact pricing version used, so TotalCost is fully derivable.

data.Title: Citation requires driver, violation, jurisdiction
data.Description: A citation must reference exactly one Driver, one ViolationType, and one issuing Jurisdiction; none may be null at intake.

data.Title: Issue date present and not future-dated
data.Description: Every citation records an IssueDate that is on or before today; a future issue date is rejected at intake.

data.Title: Driver response is pay-or-contest before the deadline
data.Description: The driver's response is one of {pay, contest} and must be recorded on or before the computed ResponseDeadline.

data.Title: Response deadline derives from jurisdiction DaysToRespond
data.Description: ResponseDeadline = IssueDate + the issuing jurisdiction's DaysToRespond; it recomputes whenever the rule changes.

data.Title: Citation status is derived, never stored
data.Description: CitationStatus (Issued -> Responded -> InContest -> Adjudicated -> Closed) is computed on read from facts and child CaseEvents; no stored status column is authoritative.

data.Title: Status order is monotonic
data.Description: A citation may only advance forward through its lifecycle states; backward transitions require an explicit, logged override.

data.Title: Contesting requires a timely contest response
data.Description: The contest track only opens when DriverResponse = contest was recorded before the ResponseDeadline.

data.Title: Contest track derives from the latest hearing
data.Description: ContestStatus (NotContested | HearingRequested -> Scheduled -> Heard) is derived from the most recent Hearings row for the citation.

data.Title: Determination decides liability
data.Description: A hearing outcome of dismissed sets liability to none and zero fine owed; upheld confirms the original fine and points.

data.Title: Hearing requires a requested contest
data.Description: A Hearings row may only be created for a citation whose ContestStatus is HearingRequested or later.

data.Title: Scheduling is manager-or-admin only
data.Description: Only manager or admin roles may set a hearing's ScheduledDate; representatives request, they do not schedule.

data.Title: Payment track derives from payments and pay-by date
data.Description: PaymentStatus (Pending -> Due -> Late -> Collections | Paid | NotOwed) is computed from Payments rows and the jurisdiction pay-by date.

data.Title: Late penalty applies after the pay-by date
data.Description: Once the pay-by date passes with no full payment, the jurisdiction's LatePenaltyPercent is added to AmountOwed.

data.Title: Paid in full closes the case
data.Description: When recorded Payments equal AmountOwed, PaymentStatus becomes Paid and the citation advances toward Closed.

data.Title: Base fine comes from the violation and jurisdiction
data.Description: The base fine is the ViolationType base amount adjusted by the jurisdiction's fee schedule; it is data, not a constant.

data.Title: Active points roll up across a driver's citations
data.Description: A driver's ActivePoints is the sum of points from non-dismissed, non-expired citations under each jurisdiction's point window.

data.Title: License track follows jurisdiction thresholds
data.Description: LicenseStatus (Valid -> Warning -> Suspended) is derived by comparing ActivePoints against the jurisdiction's warning and suspension thresholds.

data.Title: Work queue is computed from deadlines and status
data.Description: WorkQueueItems are derived from overdue responses, due/late payments, and upcoming hearings, bucketed by urgency; nothing is hand-enqueued.

data.Title: Every state-affecting change is logged
data.Description: Each status change, override, payment, and edit writes an append-only AuditLogEntries (and where lifecycle-relevant, a CaseEvents) row.

data.Title: The log never contradicts the derived state
data.Description: The append-only history explains how current computed state was reached; it is additive narration, never an alternate source of truth.

data.Title: Assistant answers are grounded in live data
data.Description: The assistant answers about a citation/rule/portal from live vw_* reads and the rulebook, recording each exchange as an AssistantTurns row.

data.Title: Every turn is cost-accounted
data.Description: Each AssistantTurns row records input/output tokens and a computed cost via ModelPricingVersions for the turn's AiModel.

data.Title: Jurisdiction knobs are editable data
data.Description: Deadlines, fines, penalty percentages, and point thresholds live in Jurisdictions/JurisdictionRules and every dependent value re-derives on edit.

data.Title: Each jurisdiction rule cites its source
data.Description: A JurisdictionRules row links to the JurisdictionSourceDocuments / ReferenceDocuments authority that justifies it.

data.Title: Four canonical roles govern access
data.Description: Exactly four roles exist (admin, manager, representative, external-llm) and every permission decision references one of them.

data.Title: CRUD is per role, per table, per field
data.Description: What each role may create/read/update/delete is data at the table and field grain; the enforced API consults it on every operation.

data.Title: Row access follows per-role WHERE policies
data.Description: Each role-table pair carries a WHERE policy; a representative sees only their own citations while admin sees all, enforced on every vw_* read.

data.Title: Lower-trust roles get redacted projections
data.Description: Fields denied to a role (e.g. PII for external-llm) are stripped from responses at projection time, not nulled afterward.

data.Title: Navigation is derived from PlatformNaviation rows
data.Description: The sidebar and route tree render from PlatformNaviation filtered by the current role's RoleVisibility; there is no hand-maintained menu.

data.Title: Route order and pinning are data
data.Description: NavLevel, SortOrder, and PinToTop on PlatformNaviation determine ordering and which screens float to the top.

data.Title: Identity is resolved per request from the magic-link session
data.Description: Each request resolves to an AppUsers row and role via the MagicLinkConfig flow; identity feeds every RBAC and scoping decision.

data.Title: Surfaces are gated by license/active flags
data.Description: Packages, features, tables, and routes carry IsLicensed/IsActive flags; unlicensed surfaces are revealed as upgrade prompts, not deleted.

data.Title: The rulebook JSON is the single source of truth
data.Description: All substrates (Postgres, Python, explain-dag, rulespeak) are mechanically derived from the rulebook by effortless build; edits go to the rulebook.

data.Title: The platform describes itself
data.Description: ERBTables/ERBFields/ERBFeatures/ERBPackages catalog every table, field, capability, and package so the app can render its own model.

data.Title: Build is generate + load; drift is guarded
data.Description: effortless build regenerates DDL/views/inserts and reloads the DB; a drift guard warns before a rebuild would clobber un-saved DB edits.

data.Title: Domain terms are defined as data
data.Description: Every domain term used in a feature or rule is defined in GlossaryTerms under a GlossaryCategories group.

data.Title: Determination is recorded
data.Description: A hearing determination (upheld / dismissed / reduced) must be recorded on the Citation before the citation-lifecycle may advance to adjudicated, and it writes a CaseEvents entry.

data.Title: Dismissal reverses consequences
data.Description: When a determination dismisses a Citation, any assessed license points are reversed and any paid amount is refunded, restoring the Driver to make-whole state.

data.Title: Lifecycle transitions are guarded
data.Description: Every state transition is gated by its guard condition and the firing role; the state-machine backbone rejects illegal transitions and logs each accepted one to CaseEvents.

data.Definition: The cited party — the person who received the traffic citation and whose license points roll up across their citations.
A line agent who owns cases and completes domain workflows on behalf of, or alongside, the driver.
A supervisor who schedules hearings and reviews/approves submissions.
A traffic ticket: one driver, one violation type, one issuing jurisdiction, an issue date, and the case hub from which all four status tracks are derived.
The regulatory authority (county or city) that issued a citation. Its row holds every deadline, fine, penalty, and point threshold that governs cases under it.
A contest event: requested by the driver, scheduled by a manager, and concluded with an outcome (e.g. Dismissed) that drives liability.
The pattern of expressing regulations as data on the Jurisdictions table, so changing a value re-derives every dependent citation with no code change.
The top-level computed track: Issued → Responded → InContest → Adjudicated → Closed.
The hearing track: NotContested | HearingRequested → Scheduled → Heard.
The money track: Pending → Due → Late → Collections | Paid | NotOwed.
The driver track: Valid → Warning → Suspended, driven by active points against jurisdiction thresholds.
What happens when a citation's response window passes with no response — it is adjudicated against the driver automatically.
The base monetary penalty for a violation, taken from the violation type's BaseFineUsd.
An additional charge of LatePenaltyPct of the fine, applied when payment misses the pay-by date.
The terminal payment state reached when a citation remains unpaid beyond the jurisdiction's DaysLateToCollections window.
Demerit points attached to a violation type; they accumulate on the driver and can trigger a license warning or suspension.
The recorded outcome of a contested citation after a hearing — upheld, reduced, or dismissed — which advances the citation-lifecycle to its adjudicated state.
The formal ruling on a contest, captured on the Citation and rendered into a determination letter from the jurisdiction template.
A recognized legal basis for disputing a citation (signage, malfunction, emergency, calibration, mistaken identity), drawn from the ContestGrounds catalog per ViolationType.
The per-(jurisdiction, violation) base fine and surcharge percentage in FeeSchedules that drives a citation's amount due.
A per-jurisdiction window (contest, payment, appeal) in DeadlineRules that determines a citation's computed deadlines and late penalties.
A percentage added to the base fine per jurisdiction, contributing to the total amount due on a citation.
The prioritized list of non-terminal items (deadlines, hearings, payments) a role must act on, scoped by that role's WHERE policy.
A data-defined lifecycle (citation-lifecycle, contest-track, payment-track, license-track) of states and guarded transitions enforced by the backbone.
The condition (referencing a business rule) that must hold for a state transition to fire, plus the role allowed to fire it.
Role-Based Access Control: the role->table->field CRUD model plus WHERE policies and redaction that govern who can do what.
Derived Postgres predicates that scope each role's visible rows (e.g. a representative sees only assigned citations), compiled from WHERE policies.
Masking of PII fields (license number, address) for roles like external-llm at the moment a row leaves the database.
Per-package/feature/table/route entitlement toggles that add or remove surfaces from the platform without code changes.
The visual inference graph showing how a calculated field (e.g. a contest deadline) is derived from raw inputs and lookups.
A context-aware chat that answers grounded in the current screen, role, and row, respecting permissions and redaction, with per-turn cost telemetry.
Per-turn records of assistant/OCR token usage and dollar cost, rolled up by model, role, and case for the cost dashboard.
A numbered TT-* record stating a precise platform rule, linked from the features, transitions, and tests that implement and verify it.
A per-state law governing fines, surcharges, deadlines, and point schedules, sourced from JurisdictionRules.
The public self-service surface where a driver looks up their own citation and chooses to pay or contest.
The official outcome document generated from a hearing determination using the jurisdiction's template.

schema.name: Depth
schema.Description: Calculated — nesting depth in the menu tree. 0 = top-level (no parent); otherwise the number of dot-segments in the dotted RouteKey above the root (e.g. 'library.rules.detail' = 2). Drives N-deep indentation.

schema.name: FullPath
schema.Description: Calculated — canonical role-agnostic URL path (equals Route). The SPA renders this under each permitted role as /:role + FullPath and resolves it only when that role's *CanRead is true; the role is an implicit prefix, never stored on the row.

schema.name: HandlerBaseName
schema.Description: Calculated — space-delimited form of the dotted RouteKey (dots and hyphens become spaces), e.g. 'library rules detail'. The client PascalCases this and prefixes the viewer role to derive the handler component deterministically: {Role} + PascalCase(HandlerBaseName) -> e.g. AdminLibraryRulesDetail, RepresentativeLibraryRulesDetail. No per-role handler is stored; edge cases get their own single-role route row instead.

data.Description: Portfolio overview: open citations, due payments, upcoming hearings.
All citations across drivers and jurisdictions — the case hub.
A single citation with its four computed status tracks, events, hearings and payments.
Contest hearings: requested, scheduled, and heard.
Payments recorded against citations.
Drivers and their rolled-up license points and status.
The rules engine: every deadline, fine, penalty and threshold as data.
Everything that needs attention now, bucketed by urgency.
Ask questions about a citation, the rules, or the portal.
Glossary, business rules, and jurisdiction source documents.
Plain-language definitions grouped by category.
The R1..R38 rules grouped by category, each linked to its schema location.
AI-distilled rule summaries per jurisdiction, with sources.
Statutes and appeal-board decisions backing the rules.
Platform administration: schema catalog, features, navigation, audit, branding.
The feature catalog with status, category and cartoon imagery.
A single feature: description, rules implemented, status, and image.
Every table in the rulebook with its per-role CRUD grants.
This very navigation tree, edited as data.
Append-only history of every change, override, and decision.
Site title, colors, logos, and contact info.
Release history of the rulebook itself.
Catalog of violation codes with base fines and point values.
The append-only lifecycle event log across all citations.
Request a hearing to contest a citation before its deadline.
Pay a citation's fine online and close the case.
Reference view of every violation code, fine, and point value.
Per-model assistant spend and token volume over time.
The AI models and pricing versions the assistant can use.
The canonical four roles and their summaries.
App-users directory: create users and assign roles.
Per-role, per-table, per-field CRUD and row-scoping policy editor.
Enable/disable and license whole packages of features and tables.
Self-describing fields catalog: every column, type, and description.
Drag-and-drop editor for the navigation tree and per-route access.
Registry of every custom (non-CRUD) API endpoint and its access.
View and edit the four lifecycle state machines and transitions.
Click any calculated field to trace its derivation graph.
The rulebook-first build pipeline, version history, and drift guard.
Hand-authored SQL customizations layered onto the generated substrate.

schema.name: Code

schema.name: DaysToRespond
schema.Description: Number of days after issuance the driver has to respond (pay or request a hearing) before default judgment.

schema.name: DaysToPayAfterRuling
schema.Description: Number of days after a guilty ruling (or after issuance if not contested) the fine is due before it is late.

schema.name: LatePenaltyPct
schema.Description: Percentage surcharge added to the assessed amount once a payment is late (e.g. 0.25 = 25%).

schema.name: DaysLateToCollections
schema.Description: Number of days a payment may remain late before it is referred to collections.

schema.name: PointSuspensionThreshold
schema.Description: Total active license points at or above which a driver's license is suspended in this jurisdiction.

schema.name: PointWarningThreshold
schema.Description: Total active license points at or above which a driver receives an advisory warning (below suspension).

schema.name: TrafficSchoolPointCap

schema.name: ParentJurisdiction
schema.Description: The parent jurisdiction in the hierarchy (e.g., US for California). Empty for root jurisdictions like countries.

schema.name: ChildJurisdictions

data.Title: California Vehicle Code — Arraignment & Bail Deadlines
data.ExtractedText: A defendant cited for an infraction must appear or pay bail within the time set by the court, generally within 21 days of the notice to appear. Failure to appear may result in a civil assessment and referral for collection.
data.Notes: Source for CA-LA DaysToRespond = 21 and the default-judgment behavior.

data.Title: Los Angeles County Traffic School Eligibility Guidance
data.ExtractedText: Eligible drivers may attend traffic school once every 18 months to mask one point from a qualifying moving violation. Commercial license holders and certain violations are excluded.

data.Title: New York Vehicle & Traffic Law — Point System
data.ExtractedText: The NYS driver point system assigns points by violation. Accumulating 11 points within 18 months may result in license suspension; the DMV may also act on fewer points for serious offenses.
data.Notes: Source for NY-NYC PointSuspensionThreshold = 11 and PointWarningThreshold = 6.

data.Title: Texas Transportation Code — Speed & Signal Offenses
data.ExtractedText: An operator shall comply with posted speed limits and traffic-control signals. A defendant must respond to a citation within the period stated on the notice, commonly 14 days, or a warrant and additional fees may issue.
data.Notes: Source for TX-AUS DaysToRespond = 14 and signal/speed violation definitions.

data.Title: Travis County Appeal Board Decision 2025-114 (Radar Calibration)
data.ExtractedText: Where the issuing agency cannot produce radar/lidar calibration records upon timely request, the speed measurement is unreliable and the citation must be dismissed. Calibration logs must be retained and produced at hearing.
data.Notes: Directly supports the dismissal of citation TC-2026-0003 (Carlos Mendez) at hearing on 2026-04-15.

data.Title: 21-Day Response Window
data.Description: An infraction defendant must appear or post bail within 21 days of the notice to appear; otherwise a civil assessment and collections referral may follow.
data.AiVersion: In LA County you have 21 days from your ticket date to pay or ask for a hearing. Miss it and the court can decide against you and add a collection fee.

data.Title: Traffic School Masks One Point
data.Description: An eligible driver may attend traffic school once per 18 months to mask one point from a qualifying moving violation.
data.AiVersion: In LA County you can take traffic school once every 18 months to hide one point from your record.

data.Title: 11-Point Suspension Threshold
data.Description: Accumulating 11 points within 18 months may result in license suspension; a warning is appropriate well before that.
data.AiVersion: In NYC, 11 points in 18 months can get your license suspended — we flag a warning at 6.

data.Title: 14-Day Response Window
data.Description: A defendant must respond to a citation within the period stated on the notice, commonly 14 days, or a warrant and additional fees may issue.
data.AiVersion: In Travis County you usually have 14 days to respond to a ticket before a warrant and extra fees can be added.

data.Title: No Calibration Records → Dismissal
data.Description: If the agency cannot produce radar/lidar calibration records on timely request, the speed reading is unreliable and the citation must be dismissed.
data.AiVersion: In Travis County, if the police can't show their radar was calibrated, a speeding ticket can be thrown out — exactly what happened on TC-2026-0003.

data.MetaDescription: A case-management portal for responding to, contesting, and resolving traffic citations across multiple jurisdictions — where every deadline, fine, and penalty is a rule, not code.
data.Tagline: Every ticket, every deadline, every rule — in one place.
data.SubTagline: Four state machines and a multi-jurisdiction rules engine, working for the driver.

data.UserMessage: What's the deadline to contest citation TC-2026-0002?
data.AssistantReply: Citation TC-2026-0002 was issued 2026-05-20 in Los Angeles County, which allows 21 days to respond — so the deadline is 2026-06-10. Requesting a hearing now (today is 2026-05-25) is well within the window.

data.UserMessage: On what basis can TC-2026-0003 be dismissed?
data.AssistantReply: Under Travis County Appeal Board decision AB-2025-114, if the agency cannot produce radar/lidar calibration records on timely request, the speed measurement is unreliable and the citation must be dismissed. The hearing notes confirm calibration records were not produced, so dismissal is supported.

data.UserMessage: How much does Carlos owe now on TC-2026-0004?
data.AssistantReply: TC-2026-0004 has a base fine of $275 (Travis County red-light). The pay-by date passed on 2026-03-01, so Travis County's 30% late penalty applies: $275 × 1.30 = $357.50. If it stays unpaid past the 45-day collections window, it moves to Collections.

data.UserMessage: How many of our citations are currently unpaid and overdue?
data.AssistantReply: As of 2026-06-06, one citation is overdue on response (TC-2026-0001) and one is late on payment heading toward collections (TC-2026-0004). The work queue lists both as urgent.

data.Title: Citations & Contests

data.Title: Core Platform
data.Description: Cross-cutting platform machinery shared by all packages: the rulebook-driven permission model, role-specific schemas, the enforced CRUD API, row-level security, the generic state-machine backbone, the work queue, and dev-ops/build tooling.

data.Title: Platform Meta & Knowledge Base
data.Description: The self-describing meta layer you usually keep hidden: the feature catalog and statuses, the table/field schema catalog, the custom API-endpoint registry, ERB versions, customizations, the glossary, and the business-rule library. Turn this off and the entire "how the platform describes itself" surface disappears, leaving just the working app.

data.Title: Roles & User Management
data.Description: The role/user administration surface: the Roles catalog and the AppUsers directory, plus the role/user admin pages and the permission model (role→table→field CRUD, per-role WHERE policies, role-specific schemas, derived RLS). Turn it OFF and an admin temporarily loses the management tools — a quick way to see the simpler app a non-admin sees. The underlying RLS keeps enforcing in Postgres regardless; this only hides the management UI.

data.Title: State Machine Admin
data.Description: The state-machine configuration surface: the StateMachines / MachineStates / StateTransitionRules / StateTransitions tables and the admin routes that view and edit them (machines, states, transition rules, transition log). Turn it off to hide the entire state-machine admin UI; the machines still drive entity lifecycles in Postgres.

data.Title: Assistant & Cost Analytics
data.Description: The portal assistant (the chat-agent state machine) plus its cost/usage telemetry: the AI model catalog (AiModels), the versioned model price book (ModelPricingVersions), and per-turn AssistantTurns rows whose token costs roll up to accounts and records. Turn it off to hide the assistant analytics surface; the assistant route still functions.

data.Title: Citation Intake
data.Description: Record a new traffic citation — driver, violation type, issuing jurisdiction, issue date — and immediately derive its response deadline from the jurisdiction's DaysToRespond rule.
data.SourceText: Records a new traffic citation as a row in the Citations table -- driver (FK to Drivers), violation type (FK to ViolationTypes), issuing jurisdiction (FK to Jurisdictions), and issue date -- and immediately derives the response deadline from the jurisdiction's DaysToRespond rule via vw_citations. An admin or representative captures the citation off a paper ticket and the computed ResponseDeadline column appears the moment the row is saved, with no manual date math. This is the front door of the whole citation-lifecycle state machine.

data.Title: Driver Response
data.Description: Capture the driver's choice — pay now or request a hearing — and advance the citation from Issued to Responded before the response window closes.
data.SourceText: Captures the driver's choice -- pay now or request a hearing -- writing it onto the Citations row and advancing the citation from Issued to Responded in vw_citations before the response window closes. A representative records the decision on behalf of the cited driver, and the contest-track state machine branches on it. The response and its timestamp become CaseEvents rows so the audit trail explains every move.

data.Title: Citation Lifecycle Tracker
data.Description: Show the live Citation status (Issued → Responded → InContest → Adjudicated → Closed) re-derived from facts and child events — never a stored, drifting column.
data.SourceText: Shows the live Citation status (Issued -> Responded -> InContest -> Adjudicated -> Closed) re-derived on every read from facts and child CaseEvents rows in vw_citations -- never a stored, drifting column. A user opens a citation and sees a glowing status path that always reflects current reality. The same computed CitationStatus column drives the work queue and the dashboards.

data.Title: Contest Tracker
data.Description: Derive the contest track (NotContested | HearingRequested → Scheduled → Heard) from the latest hearing, driving liability downstream.
data.SourceText: Derives the contest track (NotContested | HearingRequested -> Scheduled -> Heard) from the latest Hearings row, surfacing it as a calculated column on vw_citations that drives liability downstream. A representative tracking a contested ticket sees exactly where the hearing process stands. The track feeds the determination logic that decides whether a fine is owed.

data.Title: Payment & Penalty Tracker
data.Description: Compute the payment track (Pending → Due → Late → Collections | Paid | NotOwed) including the jurisdiction-specific late penalty once the pay-by date passes.
data.SourceText: Computes the payment track (Pending -> Due -> Late -> Collections | Paid | NotOwed) on vw_citations, including the jurisdiction-specific late penalty once the pay-by date passes, by joining Citations, Payments, and the jurisdiction's penalty rule. A representative or driver sees the live amount owed and its escalation status. The Late and Collections transitions are the ones the deadline-driven work queue watches most.

data.Title: License Points Monitor
data.Description: Roll up a driver's active points and drive the license track (Valid → Warning → Suspended) against each jurisdiction's warning and suspension thresholds.
data.SourceText: Rolls up a driver's active points across their Citations and drives the license track (Valid -> Warning -> Suspended) against each jurisdiction's warning and suspension thresholds, exposed on vw_drivers. A manager reviewing a driver sees the points gauge and the resulting license standing. The aggregation re-runs on every read so a dismissed citation instantly lowers the active-point total.

data.Title: Jurisdiction Rules Engine
data.Description: Edit a jurisdiction's deadlines, fines, penalty percentages, and point thresholds as data — and watch every citation under it re-derive instantly, with no code change and no migration.
data.SourceText: Lets an admin edit a jurisdiction's deadlines, fines, penalty percentages, and point thresholds as data in the Jurisdictions and JurisdictionRules tables -- and every citation under it re-derives instantly through vw_citations with no code change and no migration. A manager turns a single dial and the whole region's computed deadlines and penalties move. This is the rules-as-data thesis of the entire platform.

data.Title: Hearing Scheduling
data.Description: Request a hearing, let a manager schedule it, and record the outcome — driving the contest track and the citation's liability.
data.SourceText: Requests a hearing, lets a manager schedule it, and records the outcome as a Hearings row, driving the contest track and the citation's liability on vw_citations. A representative requests, a manager assigns a date, and the recorded outcome flows back to determine whether a fine survives. Each step writes a CaseEvents row so the timeline is complete.

data.Title: Online Payment
data.Description: Pay a citation's fine online in full, record the payment, and close the case — the path Aisha Khan took on citation TC-2026-0005.
data.SourceText: Pays a citation's fine online in full, records a Payments row, and closes the case on vw_citations -- the path Aisha Khan took on citation TC-2026-0005. A representative or driver completes checkout and the payment track flips to Paid. The closing transition is logged to CaseEvents and removes the item from the work queue.

data.Title: Work Queue
data.Description: A single prioritized list of everything needing attention now — overdue responses, due payments, upcoming hearings — bucketed by urgency.
data.SourceText: Surfaces a single prioritized list of everything needing attention now -- overdue responses, due payments, upcoming hearings -- as WorkQueueItems rows bucketed by urgency, computed from the deadline columns on vw_citations. A representative starts their day here and works top-down by urgency. Each queue item links back to the citation, driver, and the rule that put it there.

data.Title: Audit Trail
data.Description: Every status change, override, and edit on a citation is logged append-only — a full history that sits alongside, and explains, the computed current state.
data.SourceText: Logs every status change, override, and edit on a citation append-only into AuditLogEntries and CaseEvents, a full history that sits alongside -- and explains -- the computed current state in vw_citations. A manager investigating a dispute opens the trail and sees exactly who changed what and when. The append-only log never contradicts the derived state; it narrates how the state got there.

data.Title: AI Case Assistant
data.Description: Ask plain-language questions about a citation, the rules, or the portal and get an answer — with input/output token usage and per-model cost tracked for every turn.
data.SourceText: Answers plain-language questions about a citation, the rules, or the portal by writing AssistantTurns rows, with input/output token usage and per-model cost tracked for every turn against AiModels and ModelPricingVersions. A representative asks 'what's my deadline on TC-2026-0002?' and gets a grounded answer plus a logged cost. Every turn is auditable and billable down to the token.

data.Title: Glossary
data.Description: Plain-language definitions of every term in the domain — citation, jurisdiction, contest, collections, points — grouped by category.
data.SourceText: Presents plain-language definitions of every domain term -- citation, jurisdiction, contest, collections, points -- as GlossaryTerms rows grouped by GlossaryCategories. Any user clicks a term in the UI and reads what it means in this platform. The glossary is the canonical vocabulary that features and rules are written against.

data.Title: Business Rules Browser
data.Description: Browse the full set of business rules (R1..R38) grouped by category, each linked to exactly where in the schema it is enforced.
data.SourceText: Browse the full set of business rules (R1..R38) grouped by category, each linked to exactly where in the schema it is enforced.

data.Title: Jurisdiction Document Library
data.Description: Browse the source documents (appeal-board decisions, statutes) and AI-distilled rule summaries backing each jurisdiction's behavior.
data.SourceText: Browses the source documents (appeal-board decisions, statutes) and AI-distilled rule summaries backing each jurisdiction, stored as JurisdictionSourceDocuments and ReferenceDocuments rows linked to Jurisdictions. A representative researching a contest reads the actual authority behind a deadline or penalty. It closes the loop between the rules-as-data engine and the legal text that justifies each row.

data.Title: Multi-Jurisdiction Dashboard
data.Description: A top-level dashboard comparing the three jurisdictions side by side — response windows, fines, penalties, point thresholds — so the rules engine is visible at a glance.
data.SourceText: Aggregates citation volume, contest rates, payment status, and average points by jurisdiction into a dashboard reading from vw_citations and vw_drivers across all Jurisdictions rows. A manager compares CA-LA, NY-NYC, and TX-AUS at a glance and spots where deadlines or penalties are biting. Every tile is a live aggregation, never a cached snapshot.

data.Title: Canonical roles table
data.Description: Roles meta table — single queryable role vocabulary (Admin/Manager/Representative/External-LLM) with DefaultCRUD; AppUsers and the domain actor tables FK into it.
data.SourceText: Defines the canonical four-role vocabulary (admin, manager, representative, external-llm) as Roles rows that every permission, navigation, and redaction decision references. An admin manages the role catalog from one screen and the rest of the platform inherits it. There is exactly one source of truth for who the actors are.

data.Title: Role→Table→Field CRUD permission model
data.Description: Additive/subtractive CRUD permissions. Resolution per (role,table,field,op): effective = ERBFields.{Role}CRUD ?? ERBTables.{Role}CRUD ?? Roles.DefaultCRUD (field overrides table overrides role default; NULL inherits the parent). op_allowed = effective CONTAINS the C/R/U/D letter (no dashes; e.g. CRUD, RU, RD). Derived {Role}Can{Op} booleans are BLANK when the source is NULL so inherit is distinguishable from explicit deny. Seed posture: Admin/Manager DefaultCRUD=CRUD, Rep/External-LLM=R; table grants NULL; calculated/lookup/aggregation fields pinned to R, raw fields NULL.
data.SourceText: Encodes the Role -> Table -> Field CRUD permission model as data so that what each role may create, read, update, or delete is a row, not a hardcoded guard. An admin edits a grid and the enforced CRUD API changes behavior immediately. This declarative matrix is the backbone of the platform's RBAC.

data.Title: Per-role table WHERE policies
data.Description: Each ERBTables row carries a {Role}WHERE clause that limits vertical (row) access per role — the source for the derived RLS plan.
data.SourceText: Stores a per-role row-scoping WHERE policy per table so a representative sees only their own citations while an admin sees all, expressed as data the enforced API compiles into vw_* reads. An admin authors the policy once and every query for that role is filtered. Row-level security becomes configuration, not code.

data.Title: Role-specific schemas / column-limited views
data.Description: Generated vw_* views per role exposing only columns whose resolved {Role}CRUD contains R (resolved via core-role-permission-model). One column-limited view per (role,table).
data.SourceText: Generates role-specific, column-limited projections of each table so external-llm receives a redacted schema while admin gets the full set, derived from the field-level CRUD grid. A developer sees exactly the columns a role is entitled to. The same rulebook yields a different, safe view per audience.

data.Title: Enforced CRUD API
data.Description: A full CRUD API that enforces the Role→Table→Field permissions and the WHERE policies at request time.
data.SourceText: Provides the single enforced CRUD API that reads the Role -> Table -> Field permission rows and the WHERE policies before every operation, so no caller can bypass RBAC. Every app screen and every external client goes through it. It is the one door, and the door checks the rulebook.

data.Title: Derived row-level security plan
data.Description: Derived RLS: one CREATE POLICY per (role,table) built from ERBTables.{Role}WHERE (and APIEndpoints.WhereClause for custom endpoints). NULL WHERE = no row restriction. Source for basic row-level security.
data.SourceText: Compiles the per-role WHERE policies into a derived row-level-security plan that the enforced API applies to every vw_* read, turning declarative scoping rows into actual filtered results. An admin reviews the plan to confirm a representative truly cannot see another rep's citations. The plan is regenerated from the rulebook, never hand-maintained.

data.Title: Custom API endpoints registry
data.Description: APIEndpoints table cataloging non-CRUD endpoints (actions/RPC) in addition to the standard CRUD operations, each with its own role visibility.
data.SourceText: Maintains a registry of every custom (non-CRUD) API endpoint as APIEndpoints rows -- method, path, subject table, role visibility, and which state machine it triggers. An admin browses the verb surface of the whole platform in one place. Loop 4 populates this registry with every traffic-ticket action.

data.Title: Self-describing schema catalog
data.Description: ERBTables/ERBFields are the self-describing catalog of every rulebook table and field. Build is ADDITIVE ONLY: on each run it ADDS newly-introduced tables/fields and UPDATEs derived columns (e.g. FieldCount) but NEVER overwrites or deletes existing rows, so hand-set permissions survive.
data.SourceText: Exposes a self-describing schema catalog built from ERBTables and ERBFields rows so the platform can render its own data model without external documentation. A developer inspects any table's fields, types, and descriptions in the UI. The catalog stays in sync because it is generated from the rulebook itself.

data.Title: Generic state-machine backbone
data.Description: Four mechanic tables (StateMachines, MachineStates, StateTransitionRules, StateTransitions) plus one polymorphic transition log unify all lifecycle/audit handling.
data.SourceText: Provides the generic state-machine backbone -- StateMachines, MachineStates, StateTransitionRules, StateTransitions, SubjectStateInstances -- that the four traffic-ticket tracks are expressed in as data. An admin defines states and guarded transitions as rows and the engine enforces them. Every lifecycle in the platform rides this one substrate.

data.Title: Unified work queue
data.Description: WorkQueueItems surfaces non-terminal subjects across the platform's lifecycle entities, ordered by urgency, via a polymorphic pointer.
data.SourceText: Implements the unified work queue as WorkQueueItems rows computed from deadline and status columns across vw_citations, vw_hearings, and vw_payments. Any role sees the prioritized list scoped to what they may act on. It is the single 'what needs me now' surface for the whole platform.

data.Title: Feature status tracking
data.Description: ERBFeatureStatuses meta table with IsActive to separate the official shared view from design-only (DESIGNED) features.
data.SourceText: Tracks the build status of every capability via ERBFeatures.Status (FK to ERBFeatureStatuses), so the feature catalog shows designed/planned/in-progress/shipped at a glance. A product owner sees exactly what is live. This very spine row is part of that catalog.

data.Title: Rulebook-first build pipeline
data.Description: effortless build regenerates postgres SQL from the rulebook SSoT; init-db applies it; catalog tables are kept in sync additively.
data.SourceText: Drives the rulebook-first build pipeline: editing the rulebook JSON and running effortless build regenerates Postgres, Python, explain-dag, and rulespeak with no hand-written substrate code. A developer changes a rule and rebuilds; every spoke updates. The pipeline is the mechanism that makes the SSoT actually single.

data.Title: Magic-link authentication
data.Description: Passwordless email-code auth issuing JWTs the API verifies; feeds the role/RLS layer.
data.SourceText: Authenticates users with passwordless email magic links configured in MagicLinkConfig, resolving each request to an AppUsers identity and role. A user signs in with a code and the platform knows who they are. Identity feeds every RBAC and row-scoping decision downstream.

data.Title: Roles catalog & per-role dashboard
data.Description: The interactive Roles catalog plus a per-role dashboard: each role opens to a hub showing its user roster, its default CRUD posture, and its redacted-role flag, with inline user-add. This is the top of the RBAC cascade made editable.
data.SourceText: Gives admins a roles catalog with a per-role dashboard summarizing each role's table/field grants and visible navigation, reading Roles, the permission grid, and PlatformNaviation. An admin audits a role's full reach from one screen. It turns the abstract RBAC matrix into a legible per-role story.

data.Title: App-Users directory & user management
data.Description: A searchable directory of every AppUser, groupable by role, with create-user and a per-user detail page (profile + inherited-access view). This is the people side of access control, paired with the Roles catalog.
data.SourceText: Provides the App-Users directory backed by the AppUsers table for creating, editing, and assigning roles to users. An admin manages who can log in and what role they hold. Every identity the magic-link flow resolves lives here.

data.Title: Admin-equivalent cross-world access
data.Description: Roles flagged admin-equivalent may browse any role's URL world (/admin, /reps, /llm); everyone else is bounced to their own home world. This is what lets an administrator move freely across the three role surfaces.
data.SourceText: Grants admin (and admin-equivalent) roles cross-world access so they can read and act across every jurisdiction and every representative's caseload, enforced via the WHERE policies. An admin troubleshoots any citation regardless of owner. The breadth is itself a configured policy, not a hardcoded superuser bypass.

data.Title: Role URL worlds (/admin · /reps · /llm)
data.Description: Every role gets its own slug-prefixed URL world, so the same React app presents a different navigable surface per role. The home world, redirects, and sidebar all key off the active slug.
data.SourceText: Routes each role into its own URL world (/admin, /reps, /llm) so navigation, scoping, and chrome match the actor, driven by PlatformNaviation RoleVisibility. A representative lands in /reps and sees only their tools. The same rulebook produces a tailored app per role.

data.Title: Role impersonation (view-as-role)
data.Description: An admin can browse the platform exactly as another (redacted) role sees it by entering the External-LLM world, which sends an X-Impersonate-Role header so the API returns that role's filtered, redacted projection.
data.SourceText: Lets an admin view-as-role to see precisely what a representative or external-llm would see, applying that role's permission and WHERE policies live. An admin verifies redaction and scoping by walking in the role's shoes. It is a debugging superpower that never weakens the real policies.

data.Title: Per-role default CRUD editor
data.Description: The four-state Create/Read/Update/Delete control at the top of the cascade: each role's default posture is set with an inherit / grant / deny tri-state, and every table and field falls back to it unless overridden.
data.SourceText: Provides a per-role default CRUD editor that sets the baseline create/read/update/delete a role gets on a table before field-level overrides, stored as permission rows. An admin sets sane defaults once. Field grids then refine the exceptions.

data.Title: Per-table CRUD + WHERE policy editor
data.Description: On each table's detail page an admin sets per-role CRUD overrides and a per-role WHERE clause that limits which rows that role can see. The WHERE policies are the source for the derived row-level-security plan.
data.SourceText: A per-table CRUD + WHERE policy editor where an admin authors the row-scoping expression that filters a role's reads of that table's vw_*. An admin writes 'driver = current_user' once for representatives. The enforced API compiles it on every query.

data.Title: Per-field CRUD permission grid
data.Description: A column-level grid where each field's CRUD is granted or denied per role. Denied fields drop out of that role's column-limited views, which is how role-specific schemas are produced.
data.SourceText: A per-field CRUD permission grid that controls, column by column, what each role may read or write across every table, persisted as permission rows that the role-specific schemas derive from. An admin redacts a PII column for external-llm with one toggle. Granularity is data, not branching code.

data.Title: Role-wide PII redaction sweep
data.Description: Marking a role redacted runs a one-click sweep that bulk-denies every PII field carrying a *Redacted sibling, so the role transparently receives masked values instead of raw personal data across all tables.
data.SourceText: Runs a role-wide PII redaction sweep that flags every sensitive field (driver address, license number) and ensures the field grid denies it to lower-trust roles. An admin confirms no PII leaks to external-llm in one pass. The sweep reads the field catalog and the grid together.

data.Title: Redacted-response projection
data.Description: When a redacted role (or an impersonation/LLM session) reads data, the server swaps in *_redacted columns so personal identifiers are masked in the response body itself — not merely hidden in the UI.
data.SourceText: Produces the redacted-response projection the enforced API returns to a role, stripping denied columns from every vw_* result before it leaves the server. An external-llm receives a citation with PII columns absent, not nulled-after-the-fact. Redaction happens at projection time, deterministically.

data.Title: Derived row-level security enforcement
data.Description: The per-role WHERE policies and CRUD grants are compiled into role-scoped Postgres views and a search_path so that reads are filtered in the database itself, regardless of what the API or UI does.
data.SourceText: Enforces derived row-level security by applying the compiled WHERE-policy plan to every read, so a representative's query of vw_citations is silently scoped to their own rows. A rep simply cannot fetch another rep's data. The enforcement is generated from the rulebook, audited, and uniform.

data.Title: Live per-request identity resolution
data.Description: The JWT carries only an email; the role, redaction flag, and licensing visibility are looked up live from AppUsers on every request, so revoking or re-roling a user takes effect immediately without re-issuing tokens.
data.SourceText: Resolves live per-request identity from the magic-link session to an AppUsers row and role on every call, feeding RBAC and scoping with the actual current actor. The platform always knows who is asking right now. Stale or assumed identity is never used.

data.Title: Route Designer (drag-drop nav tree)
data.Description: A live, drag-and-drop editor for the entire platform navigation tree: reparent and reorder routes, edit their fields inline, create and delete routes, and set a per-route CRUD matrix — then SAVE promotes the tree back to the rulebook.
data.SourceText: Offers a drag-and-drop Route Designer that edits the PlatformNaviation tree -- parent/child, level, role-visibility, pin-to-top -- as data that the live sidebar renders. An admin reorganizes the whole app's navigation without touching code. The nav tree is rulebook rows end to end.

data.Title: Live DB-driven sidebar
data.Description: The sidebar is rendered entirely from the navigation table in the database: package-gated, role-filtered, with empty feature-status buckets auto-hidden and the active record's context grafted in. Edit the routes and the menu changes.
data.SourceText: Renders a live DB-driven sidebar straight from PlatformNaviation rows filtered by the current role's RoleVisibility. A user's menu always matches the rulebook and their permissions. There is no hand-maintained menu file to drift.

data.Title: Pin-to-top routes
data.Description: A per-route PinToTop flag floats important destinations to the top of the sidebar regardless of their position in the tree, so high-traffic admin pages stay one click away.
data.SourceText: Supports pin-to-top routes via a PlatformNaviation.PinToTop flag so high-traffic screens (Work Queue, Citations) float above the long tail. An admin pins the screens reps use most. Ordering is data the sidebar honors.

data.Title: By-Role / By-Package route galleries
data.Description: Two read-only regroupings of the same navigation set — one grouped by the roles that can reach each route, one by owning package — for auditing coverage at a glance; clicking jumps to that route in the designer tree.
data.SourceText: Generates By-Role and By-Package route galleries from PlatformNaviation so an admin can audit the app from either lens -- what each role sees, or what each package contributes. An admin reviews coverage gaps visually. Both galleries are projections of the same nav rows.

data.Title: Per-route access gating
data.Description: Visiting a URL a role isn't permitted to read renders an Access-Denied surface instead of the page, enforced in the shell from the same permission data that filters the sidebar — so deep links can't bypass the menu.
data.SourceText: Gates each route by RoleVisibility so navigating to a screen a role may not see returns a clean denial instead of a broken page, enforced from PlatformNaviation. A representative who edits the URL still cannot reach an admin screen. Access is checked against the rulebook on every navigation.

data.Title: Generic nav-backed pages
data.Description: Any navigation row that names a primary_view auto-renders a live, RLS-backed data grid plus a drill-down detail page — no bespoke component required. Most admin catalogs and logs are these generic pages.
data.SourceText: Renders generic nav-backed pages: any PlatformNaviation row with a PrimaryTable/PrimaryView gets a working list-or-detail screen with no bespoke component, reading vw_* directly. An admin adds a screen by adding a nav row. The frontend is a renderer of rulebook data.

data.Title: Dev quick-connect login
data.Description: A development-only login shortcut that lists @example.com demo users and issues a spoofed JWT in one click, so the team can hop between roles locally without the email-code round trip. Gated behind DEV_AUTH_ENABLED.
data.SourceText: Provides a dev quick-connect login that authenticates as any seeded AppUsers role for local testing without the full magic-link round trip. A developer hops between admin and representative in one click. It is a convenience that still resolves a real identity and real policies.

data.Title: Unlicensed-surface reveal
data.Description: The vendor-only mechanism that lets Effortless staff see surfaces marked unlicensed, while accounts see them hidden. It is the internal key behind every per-feature, per-table, and per-package licensing toggle.
data.SourceText: Reveals unlicensed surfaces in a muted state -- features, tables, or routes whose ERBPackage is not licensed are shown as upgrade prompts rather than hidden -- driven by the IsLicensed flags. A buyer sees what the next tier unlocks. Licensing is presentation data, not a deleted feature.

data.Title: SAVE: DB → rulebook → rebuild
data.Description: The promotion action behind every admin editor: it exports the live admin-config tables out of Postgres back into the rulebook JSON, runs effortless build, then re-initializes the database — turning fast in-DB edits into the new source of truth.
data.SourceText: Implements SAVE: edits made in the admin UI write back to the rulebook JSON and trigger a rebuild, so the DB -> rulebook -> rebuild loop closes from inside the app. An admin changes a deadline and the rulebook plus all substrates update. The app edits its own source of truth.

data.Title: Rulebook drift guard
data.Description: Before a SAVE overwrites the rulebook, a guard checks whether the rulebook is newer than the last build manifest; if so it blocks with a 409 until the operator explicitly forces the write, preventing a stale admin session from clobbering newer changes.
data.SourceText: Adds a rulebook drift guard that detects when the live DB diverges from the rulebook JSON and surfaces it before a rebuild silently reverts edits. An admin is warned instead of losing work. The guard protects the SSoT from accidental clobber.

data.Title: Config-export scope
data.Description: The defined set of admin-config tables the exporter promotes on SAVE — navigation, packages, roles, tables, fields, endpoints, glossary, business rules, state machines, and branding — deliberately excluding operational data, which resets to seeds on rebuild.
data.SourceText: Lets an admin choose the config-export scope -- which packages, tables, and routes ship in an exported rulebook -- so a tenant gets exactly the licensed subset. An admin exports a representative-only build. Scope is selected from the catalog, not edited by hand.

data.Title: Non-destructive permission sync
data.Description: A script that pushes the rulebook's RBAC matrix into the live database without a full destructive init-db, so permission tweaks can be applied to a running instance while leaving operational data intact.
data.SourceText: Performs non-destructive permission sync: when new tables or fields appear, default grants are added without overwriting an admin's existing customizations in the permission grid. An admin upgrades schema and keeps their RBAC edits. Sync is additive by design.

data.Title: Per-endpoint access editor
data.Description: An editor for the custom API-endpoint registry where each endpoint, grouped by its subject table, gets a role allow-list and an optional WHERE clause — extending the same role/row scoping used on tables to bespoke endpoints.
data.SourceText: A per-endpoint access editor that sets RoleVisibility on each APIEndpoints row, so a contest action is available to representatives but a refund only to managers. An admin tunes who can fire which verb. Endpoint security is a row, edited in the UI.

data.Title: Package enable/disable toggle
data.Description: A switch per package that flips its active flag so the entire UI responds — the package's routes, tables, and features disappear (or reappear) across the app at once, letting you demo the simpler product a non-admin sees.
data.SourceText: Enables or disables whole ERBPackages via an IsActive toggle, lighting up or dimming every feature, table, and route under that package at once. An admin turns the assistant package off for a tenant. The toggle cascades through the catalog.

data.Title: Package licensing toggle
data.Description: A per-package licensed flag that fences an entire package behind the vendor licensing model, so every route, table, and feature it owns is hidden from accounts while remaining visible to Effortless staff.
data.SourceText: Controls per-package licensing with an IsLicensed flag on ERBPackages that the unlicensed-reveal surface reads to gate paid capabilities. An admin sells the rules-engine package as a tier. Licensing state is data the whole UI respects.

data.Title: Per-feature licensing toggle
data.Description: The LICENSED/UNLICENSED switch on each feature card and detail page, flipping a single feature's own licensing flag independently of its package — used to fence off individual vendor-tooling features.
data.SourceText: Controls per-feature licensing via ERBFeatures.IsLicensed so individual capabilities can be gated even within a licensed package. An admin offers the AI assistant as an add-on. Granular licensing is a per-row flag.

data.Title: Per-table & per-route licensing toggles
data.Description: Licensing flags at the individual table and route level, so a single catalog table or navigation destination can be hidden from accounts without disabling its whole package — the finest grain of the licensing model.
data.SourceText: Adds per-table and per-route licensing toggles (IsLicensed on ERBTables and PlatformNaviation) so a tier can expose a screen while withholding the underlying editable table. An admin shows read-only jurisdictions to a lower tier. Every surface has its own license switch.

data.Title: Self-describing table catalog
data.Description: A browsable card grid of every rulebook table — package-gated and clickable through to each table's permission/detail page. The schema describes itself, so the catalog is generated, never hand-listed.
data.SourceText: Renders the self-describing table catalog from ERBTables rows -- name, package, field count, catalog flag, per-role CRUD -- so the platform documents its own schema. A developer browses every table and its governance from one screen. The catalog is generated, never written by hand.

data.Title: Fields catalog
data.Description: A live grid of every field across all tables — name, datatype, type (raw/calculated/lookup/relationship), and description — giving a single searchable index of the entire data model's columns.
data.SourceText: Renders the fields catalog from ERBFields rows -- table, field, type, datatype, description -- the column-level companion to the table catalog. A developer inspects exactly how each calculated or lookup field is defined. It is the field-level self-description of the rulebook.

data.Title: Explainer DAG (calculated-field provenance)
data.Description: A full-screen, interactive inference graph for any calculated field: click a value and trace its derivation back through lookups, intermediate calcs, and raw inputs — the platform showing exactly how a number was produced.
data.SourceText: Builds the Explainer DAG that visualizes calculated-field provenance -- click any computed cell and trace raw inputs -> lookups -> calcs -> aggregations -- generated from the rulebook's formula graph. A user understands exactly how ResponseDeadline or ActivePoints was derived. It makes the inference graph witnessable.

data.Title: Feature catalog grid (filterable cards)
data.Description: The Features page itself: a horizontal, filterable grid where every feature is a card with its own logo, searchable and narrowable by two orthogonal axes — lifecycle status and functional category. This very catalog is the feature.
data.SourceText: Presents the feature catalog as a filterable grid of cards from ERBFeatures, each with title, status, package, and cartoon logo. A product owner filters by status or package to see the platform's surface. This is the home of the very rows Loop 1 enriches.

data.Title: Feature detail editor
data.Description: The full-page dashboard for one feature — hero logo, stat strip, description, source narrative, rule refs, and metadata — with inline editing of title, description, status, category, sort order, rules, and sources.
data.SourceText: A feature detail editor for one ERBFeatures row -- title, description, SourceText, RuleRefs, status, route, image -- writing back to the rulebook. An admin documents a capability in place. The editor maintains the provenance spine this loop seeds.

data.Title: Feature status & category editors
data.Description: Drag-reorderable editors for the two feature vocabularies — lifecycle statuses and functional categories — where each bucket's title, description, ordering, and flags are edited inline, including a per-status page that reorders the features within it.
data.SourceText: Provides editors for the feature vocabulary -- ERBFeatureStatuses and ERBFeatureCategories rows -- so the controlled lists behind the catalog are themselves editable data. An admin renames a status or adds a category. The taxonomy is rulebook-managed.

data.Title: Cartoon feature-logo system
data.Description: The convention that gives every feature its own cartoon logo by id (/feature-logos/<id>.png), with a graceful category-emoji fallback when an image is missing, plus a script that crops and resizes generated art to the standard tile.
data.SourceText: Runs the cartoon feature-logo system that attaches a generated ImageUrl to each ERBFeatures row for the catalog grid. An admin regenerates a logo and the card updates. Imagery is a per-feature data attribute.

data.Title: Glossary admin
data.Description: A drag-reorderable, inline-editable glossary of platform terms organized into categories, so the shared vocabulary lives in the rulebook and stays consistent across the app and its docs.
data.SourceText: Gives admins glossary administration over GlossaryTerms and GlossaryCategories rows so the domain vocabulary stays current and complete. An admin adds 'collections' the moment a rule references it. The glossary is edited as first-class data.

data.Title: Business-rule library admin
data.Description: A categorized, reorderable library of the platform's business rules (the R-codes features reference), edited inline and promoted to the rulebook — the human-readable companion to the formulas the engine enforces.
data.SourceText: Provides business-rule library administration over BusinessRules and BusinessRuleCategories rows -- the numbered TT-* rules that features cite via RuleRefs. An admin authors a rule and links its SchemaLocation. Loop 2 fills this library so every RuleRef resolves.

data.Title: Site-branding editor
data.Description: A single-row editor for the platform's identity — logo, colors, title, contact, and SEO — that applies the theme live as you type and serves public branding on the pre-auth login screen, then promotes to the rulebook on SAVE.
data.SourceText: A site-branding editor over the SiteBranding row -- company name, colors, logos, contact info, OG tags -- so the portal's identity is data. An admin rebrands the whole app from one screen. Branding is configuration, not CSS edits.

data.Title: Global UI settings
data.Description: Browser-level workspace preferences exposed on the System Config page — such as toggling calculated-field provenance glyphs and compact table density — stored locally so each operator can tune their own view.
data.SourceText: Exposes global UI settings (theme, density, defaults) backed by branding and meta rows that the frontend reads at boot. An admin tunes the look and feel for every user. Settings are stored, not compiled.

data.Title: State-machine admin (visual + edit)
data.Description: An admin surface for the generic state machines that drive entity lifecycles: a read-only flow diagram and transition log on one tab, and forms to edit machines, states, and transition rules on another.
data.SourceText: Offers visual state-machine administration -- view and edit StateMachines, MachineStates, and StateTransitionRules rows -- for the four traffic-ticket tracks. An admin adds a guarded transition as data. Loop 5 completes these machines.

data.Title: Entity lifecycle simulator
data.Description: An operational tool that walks a record forward and backward through its lifecycle state machine, so an operator can advance, replay, or correct a record's stage and see the resulting transitions logged.
data.SourceText: Provides an entity lifecycle simulator that walks a sample citation through its state machine -- firing transitions, checking guards, logging StateTransitions -- to validate the model before real data. A developer dry-runs the citation lifecycle. It proves the machine is well-formed.

data.Title: Portal assistant (context-aware chat)
data.Description: A floating chat assistant that reads the current URL context (record, account, related entity) and runs a classify-then-answer flow grounded in that context, with an inspector that shows exactly what context it used.
data.SourceText: The context-aware portal assistant chats about the current screen, citation, or rule, logging each exchange as AssistantTurns rows with model and cost. A user asks the assistant about whatever they are looking at. It grounds answers in the live vw_* data and the rulebook.

data.Title: Cost-management dashboard
data.Description: An analytics dashboard that rolls up assistant spend by model, account, record, and turn kind, backed by a versioned model price book — with drill-downs into per-account, per-record, and per-model detail.
data.SourceText: A cost-management dashboard aggregating AssistantTurns by model and day against ModelPricingVersions to show spend and token volume. A manager watches assistant cost trend over time. Every figure is a live aggregation of logged turns.

data.Title: Per-turn cost telemetry
data.Description: Every assistant interaction is persisted as an AssistantTurns row with its token usage and computed cost, which then rolls up to the owning record and account — the data foundation the cost dashboard reads.
data.SourceText: Captures per-turn cost telemetry on each AssistantTurns row -- input tokens, output tokens, model, computed cost via ModelPricingVersions -- so spend is attributable to the turn. A developer audits a single expensive exchange. Cost is a stored, derived fact per turn.

data.Title: OCR cost estimator
data.Description: An interactive planning tool with draggable dividers and sliders for modeling OCR routing and token allocation, so the cost of extracting a document set can be estimated before the work is run.
data.SourceText: An OCR cost estimator that projects the token and dollar cost of processing a batch of citation images before running it, using AiModels and ModelPricingVersions rates. A manager budgets an intake batch up front. The estimate uses the same pricing rows that bill real turns.

schema.name: ERBTableId
schema.Description: PK = the table name as it appears in the rulebook.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Echoes ERBTableId.

schema.name: TableName
schema.Description: Table name.

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Table description (copied from the rulebook; refreshed additively by build).

schema.name: ERBPackage
schema.Description: FK -> ERBPackages.ERBPackageId. The package that owns this table.

schema.name: Platform
schema.Description: FK -> Platforms.PlatformId. The platform this table belongs to.

schema.name: PackageIsActive
schema.Description: Lookup: ERBPackage.IsActive — is this table's owning package enabled?

schema.name: IsLicensed
schema.Description: This table's own licensing flag. When false, the table is hidden from non-Effortless users regardless of its package (used to fence off Effortless Elements meta/catalog tables). Blank/true = licensed.

schema.name: PackageIsLicensed
schema.Description: Lookup: ERBPackage.IsLicensed — is this table's owning package licensed? UI hides the table from non-Effortless users when false.

schema.name: FieldCount
schema.Description: Number of fields (refreshed by build).

schema.name: IsCatalog
schema.Description: TRUE for the meta/catalog tables themselves.

schema.name: AdminCRUD
schema.Description: Admin CRUD grant for this table (contains C/R/U/D). NULL inherits Roles.DefaultCRUD for Admin.

schema.name: AdminWHERE
schema.Description: Admin row-access policy (SQL WHERE fragment) limiting which rows of this table Admin may see. NULL = no row restriction. Source for the RLS plan.

schema.name: ManagerCRUD
schema.Description: Manager CRUD grant for this table (contains C/R/U/D). NULL inherits Roles.DefaultCRUD for Manager.

schema.name: ManagerWHERE
schema.Description: Manager row-access policy (SQL WHERE fragment) limiting which rows of this table Manager may see. NULL = no row restriction. Source for the RLS plan.

schema.name: RepresentativeCRUD
schema.Description: Representative CRUD grant for this table (contains C/R/U/D). NULL inherits Roles.DefaultCRUD for Representative.

schema.name: RepresentativeWHERE
schema.Description: Representative row-access policy (SQL WHERE fragment) limiting which rows of this table Representative may see. NULL = no row restriction. Source for the RLS plan.

schema.name: ExternalLlmCRUD
schema.Description: External LLM CRUD grant for this table (contains C/R/U/D). NULL inherits Roles.DefaultCRUD for External LLM.

schema.name: ExternalLlmWHERE
schema.Description: External LLM row-access policy (SQL WHERE fragment) limiting which rows of this table External LLM may see. NULL = no row restriction. Source for the RLS plan.

schema.name: AdminCanCreate
schema.Description: Derived: AdminCRUD contains 'C' (BLANK when AdminCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: AdminCanRead
schema.Description: Derived: AdminCRUD contains 'R' (BLANK when AdminCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: AdminCanUpdate
schema.Description: Derived: AdminCRUD contains 'U' (BLANK when AdminCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: AdminCanDelete
schema.Description: Derived: AdminCRUD contains 'D' (BLANK when AdminCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ManagerCanCreate
schema.Description: Derived: ManagerCRUD contains 'C' (BLANK when ManagerCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ManagerCanRead
schema.Description: Derived: ManagerCRUD contains 'R' (BLANK when ManagerCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ManagerCanUpdate
schema.Description: Derived: ManagerCRUD contains 'U' (BLANK when ManagerCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ManagerCanDelete
schema.Description: Derived: ManagerCRUD contains 'D' (BLANK when ManagerCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: RepresentativeCanCreate
schema.Description: Derived: RepresentativeCRUD contains 'C' (BLANK when RepresentativeCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: RepresentativeCanRead
schema.Description: Derived: RepresentativeCRUD contains 'R' (BLANK when RepresentativeCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: RepresentativeCanUpdate
schema.Description: Derived: RepresentativeCRUD contains 'U' (BLANK when RepresentativeCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: RepresentativeCanDelete
schema.Description: Derived: RepresentativeCRUD contains 'D' (BLANK when RepresentativeCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ExternalLlmCanCreate
schema.Description: Derived: External LLMCRUD contains 'C' (BLANK when External LLMCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ExternalLlmCanRead
schema.Description: Derived: External LLMCRUD contains 'R' (BLANK when External LLMCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ExternalLlmCanUpdate
schema.Description: Derived: External LLMCRUD contains 'U' (BLANK when External LLMCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: ExternalLlmCanDelete
schema.Description: Derived: External LLMCRUD contains 'D' (BLANK when External LLMCRUD is NULL = inherits role default).

schema.name: AdminIsRedacted
schema.Description: Per-table redaction override for Admin. NULL = inherit Roles.IsRedactedRole. TRUE/FALSE explicitly sets whether this role is redacted FOR THIS TABLE (drives the default field access on "<Field>Redacted"-paired fields).

schema.name: ManagerIsRedacted
schema.Description: Per-table redaction override for Manager. NULL = inherit Roles.IsRedactedRole. TRUE/FALSE explicitly sets whether this role is redacted FOR THIS TABLE (drives the default field access on "<Field>Redacted"-paired fields).

schema.name: RepresentativeIsRedacted
schema.Description: Per-table redaction override for Representative. NULL = inherit Roles.IsRedactedRole. TRUE/FALSE explicitly sets whether this role is redacted FOR THIS TABLE (drives the default field access on "<Field>Redacted"-paired fields).

schema.name: ExternalLlmIsRedacted
schema.Description: Per-table redaction override for External LLM. NULL = inherit Roles.IsRedactedRole. TRUE/FALSE explicitly sets whether this role is redacted FOR THIS TABLE (drives the default field access on "<Field>Redacted"-paired fields).

schema.name: ERBFields
schema.Description: Reverse: fields belonging to this table.

schema.name: CreatedAt
schema.Description: Audit: when this row was first inserted. Auto-stamped by the audit trigger (now() on INSERT); immutable thereafter.

schema.name: CreatedBy
schema.Description: Audit: the OWNER — the JWT user (auth.email()) who created this row. Auto-stamped on INSERT; immutable thereafter. NULL for build-time seed rows (no signed-in user).

schema.name: ModifiedAt
schema.Description: Audit: when this row was last written. Auto-stamped by the audit trigger (now() on every INSERT/UPDATE).

schema.name: ModifiedBy
schema.Description: Audit: the JWT user (auth.email()) who last wrote this row. Auto-stamped on every INSERT/UPDATE.

schema.name: ModifiedByModel
schema.Description: Audit: the AI model credited on the last save when an LLM made/assisted the change (e.g. "gpt-4o"). NULL for plain human writes. Read from the app.modified_by_model session GUC by the audit trigger. The owner (CreatedBy/ModifiedBy) is always the human JWT user, never a model.

One row per state machine governing a subject entity's lifecycle. Declaration table: defines WHICH entity transitions through a fixed set of states. SubjectTableName is a documented convention string, NOT a relationship (keeps the DAG acyclic; the subject keeps its own raw CurrentStateKey column).
The legal states of each StateMachine. One row per (machine, state). StateKey is the bare value stored in the subject's current-state column.
The legal edges (guarded transitions) of each machine — the STATIC shape. GuardDescription cites existing R-codes in prose; it does NOT reimplement rule logic.

The INSTANCE log — one row per actual transition of ANY subject. Generalizes the retired StageTransitions table. SubjectTableName/SubjectId are a polymorphic raw-string pointer (NOT relationships) so the DAG stays acyclic. The subject's raw current-state column holds 'now'; this log holds history.
Catalog of AI models the assistant can call — external and internal. Pricing lives in ModelPricingVersions.
Versioned USD-per-1M-token price book for AI models. IsActive marks the current version; superseded versions are retained for reproducible historical cost.

Calculated — canonical role-agnostic URL path (equals Route). The SPA renders this under each permitted role as /:role + FullPath and resolves it only when that role's *CanRead is true; the role is an implicit prefix, never stored on the row.

Calculated — space-delimited form of the dotted RouteKey (dots and hyphens become spaces), e.g. 'library rules detail'. No per-role handler is stored; edge cases get their own single-role route row instead.
